primer.markdown:
# One tool interface to hold every visual API

The agents (Module 02) do not call Canva or Figma or Adobe directly. They call **tools** — objects that conform to the Model Context Protocol. Every tool, regardless of what API it wraps, has the same three-part shape: a `name`, a `description` that teaches the LLM when to reach for it, and an `input_schema` that constrains what arguments it will accept. The agent-builder guide makes this literal in lines 644-662: the `MCPTool` TypeScript interface has exactly those fields plus an `execute(params)` method that returns a `ToolResult`.

The payoff is fungibility. When you swap Canva for Figma, you do not edit the agent — you edit one tool file. The guide's example at lines 664-711 shows a `CanvaAPI` class implementing `MCPTool`: its `execute` method is a plain `fetch()` to `https://api.canva.com/rest/v1/designs`. Replace that URL and body with Figma's and you have a Figma tool that the exact same agent can use. The agent never knew there was a difference.

This is why the Universal Template generalises across Canva + NanoBanana, Figma + DALL-E, Adobe + Midjourney, and Sketch + Stable Diffusion. Each combination is a different *set of tools* — but the same *set of agents*, the same system prompts, the same decision matrix, the same quality gates. The `canva-sdk-two-agent-spec.yaml` formalises this at lines 109-136: the planner gets a short list of tools (`canva_list_templates`, `canva_get_brand_kit`, `analyze_design_requirements`) while the executor gets the action tools (`nanobanana_generate`, `canva_create_design`, `canva_add_elements`, `canva_export_design`). Swap the prefix and the pattern still holds.
primer.markdown_es:
# Una interfaz de herramientas que contiene a toda API visual

Los agentes (Módulo 02) no llaman a Canva, Figma ni Adobe directamente. Llaman a **herramientas** — objetos que cumplen con el Model Context Protocol. Cada herramienta, independientemente de la API que envuelva, tiene la misma forma de tres partes: un `name`, una `description` que le enseña al LLM cuándo usarla, y un `input_schema` que restringe los argumentos. La guía del constructor de agentes lo vuelve literal en las líneas 644-662: la interfaz TypeScript `MCPTool` tiene exactamente esos campos más un método `execute(params)` que retorna un `ToolResult`.

El beneficio es la intercambiabilidad. Cuando sustituyes Canva por Figma, no editas el agente — editas un archivo de herramienta. El ejemplo de la guía en las líneas 664-711 muestra una clase `CanvaAPI` que implementa `MCPTool`: su método `execute` es un simple `fetch()` a `https://api.canva.com/rest/v1/designs`. Reemplaza esa URL y ese cuerpo por los de Figma y tienes una herramienta Figma que el mismo agente puede usar. El agente nunca supo que había una diferencia.

Por eso la Plantilla Universal se generaliza a Canva + NanoBanana, Figma + DALL-E, Adobe + Midjourney y Sketch + Stable Diffusion. Cada combinación es un *conjunto distinto de herramientas* — pero el mismo *conjunto de agentes*, los mismos prompts del sistema, la misma matriz de decisión, las mismas compuertas de calidad.
primer.key_insight: MCP is the seam. Swap the tool file, keep the agent. That is why one template covers Canva + NanoBanana, Figma + DALL-E, and every pair after.
primer.key_insight_es: MCP es la costura. Cambia el archivo de herramienta, conserva el agente. Por eso una plantilla cubre Canva + NanoBanana, Figma + DALL-E, y cada par siguiente.

visual.alt: A layered diagram with three horizontal tiers. Top tier: two agent boxes labeled 'Planning Agent' and 'Execution Agent' (same as Module 02). Middle tier: a single rectangle labeled 'MCP Tool Layer' containing four tool chips, each showing the trio (name / description / input_schema). Bottom tier: four concrete API swimlanes (Canva, Figma, Adobe Express, Sketch) each connected by dashed lines to a matching tool chip above. A caption reads: 'Swap the lane, keep the layer.'

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the MCPTool TypeScript interface (lines 644-662), then a concrete Canva implementation (lines 664-711), then a drop-in Figma rewrite shown side-by-side. Learner confirms they can identify the three invariant MCP fields (name, description, input_schema) and the one variable part (the body of execute()).

questions.prompt: Per the MCPTool interface in CLAUDE-SDK-AGENT-BUILDER-GUIDE.md lines 646-655, which field carries the description an agent reads to decide *when* to call the tool?
questions.explanation: The interface declares `description: string` alongside `name` and `input_schema`. The agent selects tools by reading `description` text; `name` is only the identifier used when actually issuing the tool-use call. `input_schema` constrains arguments, not intent.

questions.prompt: In `canva-sdk-two-agent-spec.yaml` (lines 113-136), the planner and executor agents are given *different* tool lists. Which tool is assigned to the executor and NOT to the planner?
questions.explanation: The spec at line 127 lists `nanobanana_generate` as an executor tool (it generates assets — a tactical action). The planner's tools (lines 113-116) are read-only discovery tools: list templates, fetch brand kit, analyze requirements. The split mirrors the strategic-vs-tactical division from Module 02.

questions.prompt: The CanvaAPI example at CLAUDE-SDK-AGENT-BUILDER-GUIDE.md lines 667-677 declares `name = 'canva_create_design'` and an `input_schema` requiring which two fields?
questions.explanation: Lines 670-677: the input_schema declares properties `template_id` and `title`, and `required: ['template_id', 'title']`. These are the two arguments the LLM must supply on every tool call. The implementation then posts them to the Canva REST endpoint at line 684.
